lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Bring It All Together -- Mini-presentation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari | Aula 9 -- Bring It All Together

listenings.text: Hi everyone, this is a quick message before your mini-presentations next week. I want to share a few tips so you feel ready and confident. First, always start with a clear outline, so your audience knows what is coming. Second, rehearse out loud at home at least three times; this controls your pace and calms your nerves. During the talk, slow down and use a visual aid, like one simple slide, to engage your listeners. Remember to highlight your main point clearly, and use signpost phrases to move smoothly from one idea to the next. At the end, give the audience one clear takeaway they will remember. Do not worry if you feel nervous; even experienced speakers do. Take a breath, smile, and recap your key message. Good luck, and I know you will do a great job.

Good morning, everyone. Today, I am going to talk about why I love skateboarding. Let me start with a little background. I began skating two years ago, after a friend lent me his old board. First, skateboarding keeps me active and teaches me patience, because you fall many times before you land a trick. Moving on to the social side, the skate park is where I made most of my friends. I would like to highlight one more thing: skating taught me to stay calm under pressure and to keep trying. To sum up, skateboarding is not just a hobby for me; it is a way to stay confident and connected. Thank you for listening. The key takeaway is simple: find something you love, and keep practicing every day.

extra_audio.text: First, you choose your topic and write a short outline. Then, you create one simple visual aid to support your idea. Next, you rehearse your talk out loud and check your pace. After that, you practice your signpost phrases and your main takeaway. Finally, you present with confidence and recap your key message.
